source_paper_title: Opportunity Hunters: A Model of Competitive Sequential Inspections
context:
Section 6.2.1 (Observable vs. Unobservable Actions), page 29.

This reasoning no longer holds in the parameter range in which the follower prize is large, because in this case the fact that the symmetric MPE involves mixed strategies implies that the players would be asymmetrically informed about past inspection times, which affects their strategic considerations. The characterization of MPE in this environment remains an open question.
title: Characterize Markov perfect equilibrium under unobservable inspection times in opportunity hunting
background_latex:
Consider a continuous-time two-player game in which an opportunity (a prize) arrives at a random time \(T\sim \mathrm{Exp}(\lambda)\) with parameter \(\lambda>0\). The arrival time is not directly observed by the players. Each player may choose inspection times \(0<t_1<t_2<\cdots\) (possibly random and history-dependent); each inspection costs \(c>0\). Players discount payoffs at rate \(r>0\). If a player inspects at time \(t\) and the prize has already arrived (\(T\le t\)), then the prize is discovered and the game ends: the discoverer receives \(v_1>0\) and the other player receives \(v_2\ge 0\). If an inspection occurs before the prize arrives (\(T>t\)), nothing is learned except that the prize has not yet arrived, and the game continues.

In the baseline model, inspection times are publicly observed, so players share the same history of past inspections. Under the alternative information structure of interest here, inspection times are not observed by the opponent: each player observes only her own inspection times and outcomes (which are always “failure” until the first discovery), and the game ends publicly when the prize is discovered. A Markov perfect equilibrium (MPE) refers to a (possibly mixed) strategy profile that is sequentially rational in every continuation game, with strategies conditioned only on payoff-relevant state variables; under unobservable actions the relevant state is each player’s belief (posterior) about whether the prize has arrived and about the opponent’s private inspection history/strategy-induced information.

The model parameters are \((\lambda,r,c,v_1,v_2)\). The quantitative objects characterized in the observable-actions case include (i) the equilibrium inspection-time distribution (e.g., its support and density when continuous), and (ii) the induced equilibrium payoff \(v_0\) and inspection rate/frequency (e.g., the lower support point, which equals the optimal one-player recurrent inspection time \(\tau^*\) in the diffused regime).
problem_definition_latex:
In the opportunity-hunting game described above with parameters \((\lambda,r,c,v_1,v_2)\), assume inspection times are not observable to the other player (private actions), while discovery of the prize ends the game publicly.

Characterize the Markov perfect equilibrium (existence, and when it exists, the equilibrium strategy profile and induced equilibrium payoff/inspection rate) under this unobservable-actions information structure, in the parameter regime where the follower prize is large (i.e., the regime in which the observable-actions model yields a mixed, continuously supported equilibrium inspection-time distribution).

open_problem_quote: This reasoning no longer holds in the parameter range in which the follower prize is large, because in this case the fact that the symmetric MPE involves mixed strategies implies that the players would be asymmetrically informed about past inspection times, which affects their strategic considerations. The characterization of MPE in this environment remains an open question.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The question is a constructive equilibrium-characterization problem under asymmetric information rather than a single crisp inequality that can be falsified by a finite instance. While one can numerically search for candidate equilibria in restricted strategy classes, failure to find one is not informative, so automated counterexample search is not well aligned.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: A coding-agent loop could parameterize candidate belief-based strategies (e.g., hazard-rate policies) and evaluate sequential rationality via simulation and best-response computations, providing a computable fitness signal. However, the correct state variable is an infinite-dimensional belief over the opponent’s private history, making faithful evaluation and convergence to a true MPE challenging.

verification.notice: Extracted from the source paper and passed an automated fidelity audit; not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The open problem asks for a characterization of Markov perfect equilibrium in the opportunity-hunting game when inspection times are unobservable, in the parameter regime where the observable-actions model yields a continuous mixed equilibrium supported on an interval \([\tau^*,\tau^\circ]\). In the original paper, observability plus the exponential/memoryless structure collapses the payoff-relevant state to the time since the last public inspection, yielding a unique symmetric MPE; when the follower prize is large, equilibrium is a continuous distribution solving an indifference integral equation (and an associated ODE) with endogenous continuation value \(v_0=w(\tau^*)\).

Under unobservable inspections, the key difficulty is that players no longer share a common public history of failed inspections, so the relevant state becomes a belief (or distribution) over whether the prize has arrived and over the opponent’s private inspection history. This turns the problem into a partially observed stochastic game with private actions; the Markov state is no longer one-dimensional (time since last inspection) but a belief hierarchy or sufficient statistic induced by equilibrium strategies. Existing forward-citing work identified here ("Timing Games: Probabilistic backrunning and spam") contributes tools for equilibrium existence/uniqueness in timing games with multiple action times, but it is finite-horizon and does not resolve the belief-based MPE characterization in the infinite-horizon discounted private-action setting.

Overall, based on the available forward-citation evidence, the private-inspection MPE characterization in the large-\(v_2\) (diffused) regime remains open. Promising directions include searching for a tractable sufficient statistic (e.g., a hazard-rate or posterior process) under symmetric stationary strategies, and adapting methods from partially observable stochastic games and continuous-time mixed stopping/timing games to derive and solve an equilibrium indifference condition defined on the endogenous belief state.

sources.connection:
The open problem concerns Markov perfect equilibrium in a continuous-time, infinite-horizon (discounted) two-player sequential inspection game with exponential arrival and private (unobservable) inspection times, where the relevant state is a belief over arrival and opponents private history.
